request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","name":"Parse","one_liner":"Mobile Backend-as-a-Service (MBaaS) platform for mobile developers, acquired by Facebook in 2013 and shut down in January 2017.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both charge developers/technical buyers for platform access, but Parse used usage-metered pricing aimed at individual mobile developers and app studios, while the new pitch is a licensed B2B SaaS sold to academic spinouts and small CROs in biotech. The platform-licensing model has surface overlap, but the billing mechanism (usage-metered vs. subscription license) and the buyer persona differ substantially."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Parse served the global mobile developer ecosystem (consumer app builders). The new pitch targets a narrow vertical: academic spinouts and small contract research organizations in the life sciences. These are entirely different markets with different buying cycles, regulatory considerations, and competitive landscapes."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Parse grew bottoms-up through self-service developer adoption (YC community, word-of-mouth among app developers). A biotech SaaS selling to academic spinouts and CROs requires a more consultative, relationship-driven GTM with procurement/compliance gatekeepers — almost the opposite motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are early-stage platform businesses that targeted a technically sophisticated but relatively small initial buyer pool before attempting broader scale. Parse reached 20,000 developers organically within ~1 year; the new pitch is similarly trying to plant a platform in a niche technical community first."}},"why_similar":"Both are developer/practitioner-facing infrastructure platforms that abstract away complex back-end work (cloud data management for mobile apps vs. wet-lab assay management), sold to a technically oriented but niche buyer base. Both follow a platform-licensing or usage model rather than pure services, and both started as niche tools aimed at a technically savvy early adopter community before targeting broader scale.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges on at least four concrete dimensions: (1) Sector — biotech/life sciences vs. mobile developer tooling; (2) Customer type — regulated enterprise buyers (academic spinouts, CROs) vs. individual developers; (3) Monetization — subscription/license recurring revenue vs. usage-metered cloud consumption; (4) Shutdown risk profile — Parse was killed by a corporate parent's strategic pivot (Facebook deprioritized developer tools), whereas the new pitch is an independent company without an acquirer's agenda. The new pitch also operates in a regulated environment (lab data integrity, GxP compliance) that Parse never faced.","failure_causes":["corporate-parent strategic deprioritization (Facebook acquired and then killed the product)","zero revenue alignment post-acquisition (Facebook had no incentive to monetize Parse independently)","single point of control creating existential shutdown risk","developer lock-in without sufficient migration tooling before shutdown announcement","no standalone business model after acquisition — revenue and unit economics never proven at arm's length"],"lessons_for_input":["Stay independent long enough to validate a standalone revenue model before considering acquisition — acqui-hire exits can erase the product entirely.","Build contractual and technical migration guarantees into customer agreements from day one so that any platform disruption does not strand customers.","Target regulated buyers (academic spinouts, CROs) who have long-term data retention obligations — this creates stickier retention than consumer-grade developer tools.","Establish GxP/GLP compliance features early; regulated customers will not adopt a platform that cannot pass audit, but compliance also dramatically raises switching costs once adopted.","Diversify distribution beyond a single ecosystem channel to avoid the single-parent-company shutdown risk that killed Parse."]}
